text: The speed of light in vacuum is c = 299,792,458 m/s (exact). In natural units used in particle physics, c = 1. The reduced Planck constant ℏ = 1.054571817 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s. In natural units, ℏ = 1, and energies are measured in eV or GeV.
The fine-structure constant α = e²/(4πε₀ℏc) ≈ 1/137.036. It characterizes the strength of electromagnetic interactions. At the Z boson mass scale (91.2 GeV), the running coupling is α(M_Z) ≈ 1/128.

The strong coupling constant α_s(M_Z) = 0.1179 ± 0.0009 at the Z boson mass. It runs logarithmically with energy: α_s decreases at higher energies (asymptotic freedom). At 1 GeV, α_s ≈ 0.5. The QCD scale parameter Λ_QCD ≈ 200-300 MeV.
The Fermi coupling constant G_F = 1.1663788 × 10⁻⁵ GeV⁻². The weak mixing angle sin²θ_W = 0.23122 ± 0.00003. The W boson mass M_W = 80.377 ± 0.012 GeV. The Z boson mass M_Z = 91.1876 ± 0.0021 GeV.

The Higgs boson mass is m_H = 125.25 ± 0.17 GeV (PDG 2023). It was discovered at CERN in 2012 by the ATLAS and CMS experiments. The Higgs total width is Γ_H ≈ 3.2 MeV. Dominant decay modes: H→bb̄ (58%), H→WW* (21%), H→gg (8.2%), H→ττ (6.3%), H→cc̄ (2.9%), H→ZZ* (2.6%), H→γγ (0.23%).

The top quark mass is m_t = 172.69 ± 0.30 GeV (direct measurements). It is the heaviest known elementary particle. The top quark decays almost exclusively to Wb (BR ≈ 99.8%). Its lifetime τ_t ≈ 5 × 10⁻²⁵ s is shorter than the QCD hadronization time, so it decays before forming hadrons.

The W boson mass is M_W = 80.377 ± 0.012 GeV. The W boson width is Γ_W = 2.085 ± 0.042 GeV. W bosons decay to lepton-neutrino pairs (BR ≈ 10.8% each for e, μ, τ) and quark pairs (BR ≈ 67.4% hadronic). W bosons are produced in pp collisions primarily via quark-antiquark annihilation: ud̄ → W⁺.

The Z boson mass is M_Z = 91.1876 ± 0.0021 GeV. The Z boson width is Γ_Z = 2.4952 ± 0.0023 GeV. Z decays to fermion-antifermion pairs: ee (3.36%), μμ (3.37%), ττ (3.37%), neutrinos (20.0%), hadrons (69.9%). The number of light neutrino generations N_ν = 2.9840 ± 0.0082.

Quark masses (PDG 2023): up m_u = 2.16 MeV, down m_d = 4.67 MeV, strange m_s = 93.4 MeV, charm m_c = 1.27 GeV, bottom m_b = 4.18 GeV (MS-bar), top m_t = 172.69 GeV (pole mass). Quarks carry color charge (red, green, blue) and fractional electric charge (+2/3 for u,c,t; -1/3 for d,s,b).

Lepton masses: electron m_e = 0.511 MeV, muon m_μ = 105.66 MeV, tau m_τ = 1776.86 MeV. Neutrino mass differences: Δm²₂₁ = 7.53 × 10⁻⁵ eV², |Δm²₃₂| = 2.453 × 10⁻³ eV². The tau lepton lifetime is τ_τ = 2.903 × 10⁻¹³ s.

The differential cross-section for 2→2 scattering is dσ/dΩ = |M|²/(64π²s), where M is the matrix element and √s is the center-of-mass energy. For the Drell-Yan process qq̄→ℓ⁺ℓ⁻, the leading-order cross-section is σ = (4πα²)/(3s) × Σ_q e_q² × (parton luminosity). Total pp cross-section at √s=13 TeV: σ_tot ≈ 100 mb.

The partial decay width for a particle of mass M decaying to two particles is Γ = |p|/(8πM²) × |M|² × (symmetry factors), where |p| is the magnitude of the 3-momentum of either final-state particle in the rest frame. For H→ff̄: Γ = (N_c × G_F × m_f² × m_H)/(4√2π) × β³, where β = √(1 - 4m_f²/m_H²).

The QCD running coupling at one-loop: α_s(μ²) = α_s(μ₀²)/[1 + (α_s(μ₀²)/(2π)) × b₀ × ln(μ²/μ₀²)], where b₀ = (33 - 2n_f)/6 for n_f active quark flavors. At two-loop, additional b₁ = (153 - 19n_f)/6 corrections apply. Asymptotic freedom requires b₀ > 0, satisfied for n_f ≤ 16.

The relativistic Breit-Wigner resonance formula: σ(s) = 12π/M² × Γ_i×Γ_f/[(s-M²)² + M²Γ²] × (2J+1)/((2s₁+1)(2s₂+1)), where M is the resonance mass, Γ is the total width, Γ_i and Γ_f are partial widths for initial and final states, J is the resonance spin, and s₁,s₂ are initial particle spins.

Instantaneous luminosity: L = n₁n₂f/(4πσ_xσ_y), where n₁,n₂ are particles per bunch, f is the collision frequency, σ_x,σ_y are transverse beam sizes. Integrated luminosity: L_int = ∫L dt. The number of events is N = σ × L_int. LHC Run 2 delivered ~140 fb⁻¹ per experiment at √s = 13 TeV.

ATLAS detector at the LHC: inner detector (silicon pixels + strips + TRT) for tracking in 2T solenoid field, electromagnetic calorimeter (liquid argon + lead absorber, |η|<3.2), hadronic calorimeter (steel/scintillator tile barrel + LAr endcap), muon spectrometer (toroid magnets, MDT chambers). Pseudorapidity coverage |η|<4.9 for calorimetry.

CMS detector at the LHC: all-silicon tracker (pixel + strips) in 3.8T solenoid, PbWO₄ crystal electromagnetic calorimeter (76,000 crystals), brass/scintillator hadronic calorimeter, iron/gas muon chambers. CMS uses a compact design with the solenoid encompassing tracker + calorimeters.

Electromagnetic calorimeter energy resolution: σ_E/E = a/√E ⊕ b/E ⊕ c, where a is the stochastic term (~10%/√E for sampling, ~2-3%/√E for homogeneous), b is the noise term, c is the constant term (~0.5-1%). Hadronic calorimeter resolution is typically 50-100%/√E due to invisible energy (nuclear binding, neutrinos).

Monte Carlo event generators for HEP: Pythia 8 for general-purpose pp/ee event generation including parton showers and hadronization. MadGraph5_aMC@NLO for NLO matrix element calculations. Sherpa for multi-leg NLO merged predictions. Herwig 7 for angular-ordered parton shower. POWHEG for NLO matching.

Geant4 is used for full detector simulation in HEP. It simulates particle transport through matter including electromagnetic showers, hadronic interactions, multiple scattering, and energy loss. Typical CPU time: 1-10 minutes per pp event at the LHC. GPU acceleration via Celeritas (EM physics, 3-10× speedup) and Opticks (optical photon transport, 50-200× speedup).

Delphes is a fast detector simulation framework. It parameterizes detector response using efficiency functions, energy smearing, and jet clustering rather than full Geant4 simulation. Processing time: ~0.1s per event (1000× faster than Geant4). Includes modules for tracking, calorimetry, b-tagging, and tau-tagging.

Jet algorithms: anti-kT (standard at LHC, produces circular jets), Cambridge-Aachen (angular-ordered), kT (soft particles clustered first). Typical jet radius R=0.4 for resolved jets, R=1.0 for boosted objects. Jet energy scale uncertainty at ATLAS/CMS: 1-3% for central jets. b-jet identification efficiency: ~70-80% with light-jet rejection ~100-1000.

Statistical methods in HEP: the profile likelihood ratio test statistic q_μ = -2ln(L(μ,θ̂_μ)/L(μ̂,θ̂)) where μ is the signal strength and θ are nuisance parameters. Discovery significance: p-value < 2.87×10⁻⁷ (5σ). Exclusion limits set at 95% CL using CLs method. Asymptotic approximations valid for large datasets.

The Standard Model Lagrangian: L = L_gauge + L_fermion + L_Higgs + L_Yukawa. The gauge sector is SU(3)_C × SU(2)_L × U(1)_Y with 12 gauge bosons (8 gluons, W±, Z, γ). Electroweak symmetry breaking via the Higgs mechanism gives masses to W±, Z, and fermions while leaving the photon massless.

The CKM matrix parametrizes quark flavor mixing: V_CKM = [[V_ud V_us V_ub],[V_cd V_cs V_cb],[V_td V_ts V_tb]]. Key values: |V_ud|=0.97373, |V_us|=0.2243, |V_ub|=0.00382, |V_cb|=0.0408. CP violation in the quark sector is characterized by the Jarlskog invariant J ≈ 3.18 × 10⁻⁵.

Supersymmetry (SUSY) predicts a superpartner for each SM particle differing by spin-1/2. Current LHC limits: gluino mass > 2.3 TeV, squark mass > 1.8 TeV (simplified models). No SUSY particles discovered as of 2025. The MSSM has 105 free parameters reduced to ~20 in constrained models (cMSSM, pMSSM).

Unit conversions in particle physics: 1 GeV = 1.602×10⁻¹⁰ J. ℏc = 197.327 MeV·fm. 1 barn = 10⁻²⁴ cm² = 10⁻²⁸ m². 1 fb⁻¹ = 10¹⁵ b⁻¹ = 10³⁹ cm⁻². Conversion factor: Γ(GeV) × τ(s) = ℏ = 6.582×10⁻²⁵ GeV·s. Natural units: c = ℏ = 1, [mass] = [energy] = [momentum] = GeV.

Cosmological parameters (Planck 2018): Hubble constant H₀ = 67.4 ± 0.5 km/s/Mpc. Matter density Ω_m = 0.315. Dark energy density Ω_Λ = 0.685. Baryon density Ω_b = 0.0493. CMB temperature T₀ = 2.7255 K. Age of the universe t₀ = 13.787 ± 0.020 Gyr.

Nuclear binding energy per nucleon peaks at Fe-56 (~8.79 MeV/nucleon). The semi-empirical mass formula: B(A,Z) = a_V×A - a_S×A^(2/3) - a_C×Z(Z-1)/A^(1/3) - a_A×(A-2Z)²/A + δ(A,Z). Typical values: a_V≈15.56 MeV, a_S≈17.23 MeV, a_C≈0.697 MeV, a_A≈23.29 MeV.

QCD confinement: quarks and gluons cannot be isolated as free particles. The QCD string tension is σ ≈ 1 GeV/fm ≈ 0.18 GeV². Hadronization models: Lund string fragmentation (Pythia), cluster hadronization (Herwig). The deconfinement temperature T_c ≈ 155 MeV corresponds to the quark-gluon plasma phase transition.
